body.text: INJURY PREVENTION IN PUBLIC HEALTH FACILITATOR GUIDE
Center for Injury Research and Policy Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health and
Department of Public Health Diné College
Collection of Quick Engagement Activities .......................... Error! Bookmark not defined.
INTRODUCTION
Facilitator’s Guide for Injury Prevention in Public Health

Welcome to the Injury Prevention in Public Health Facilitator’s Guide. This document is designed to support facilitators in delivering structured and engaging sessions using previously recorded videos. It provides session objectives, discussion prompts, and key takeaways to ensure a comprehensive learning experience.

These structured learning sessions were created in collaboration with Diné College and consist of 10 prepared lectures that fit into 10 courses. Each lesson plan also includes a recorded lecture introducing the basics of Injury Prevention as a field of study. Further details of the development and evaluation of this collaboration effort can be found in the publication {INCLUDE CITATION WHEN READY}.
Learning Session Structure Overview

items.text: Target Audience: Public health undergraduate and graduate programs
Duration: 70 Minutes
Format: Facilitator leading the session withtwovideo presentationsandinteractive discussions.

body.text: Overview of Suggested Courses for the Recorded Lectures

items.text: Introduction to Public Health
Recording: Risk & Protective Factors
Lecturer: Emily Kumpf, MPH, Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health
Planning, Implementing, and Evaluation Health Promotion Programs
Recording:Safety in Seconds
Lecturer:Elise Omaki, MPH, Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health
Principles of Health Education
Recording:Home Injury Prevention & CHASE
Lecturer:Wendy Shields,PhD, MPH, Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health
American Indian Maternal, Infant, Child, and Sexual Health
Recording:Reducing Disparities in Risks for Sleep-Related Infant Deaths
Lecturer:Eileen M. McDonald, Practice Professor & MSPH Program Director, Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health
Health Services & Policy
Recording:Seat Belt Policy Story
Lecturer:Theresa Chirles, PhD, MA,Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health
Introduction to Wellness
Recording:Healthy Relationships
Lecturer:Brent Crowe {Affiliation}
Environmental Health
Recording:Safe Systems
Lecturer:Shannon Frattaroli, PhD, Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health
Public Health Research Methods
Recording:Research Study Designs in Injury & Violence Prevention
Lecturer:Emily Kumpf, MPH, Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health
Community Health Assessment and Planning
Recording:Addressing the Opiod Epidemic: A community-based pilot program in rural southwest US.
Lecturer:Robert Morones, MPH, Phoenix Area Indian Health Serviceand U.S. Dept. of Health & Human Services
Mental Health Issues in Public Health
Recording:Alcohol & Substance Use
Lecturer:Natasha Oyedele, PhD, MPH, Johns Hopkins University Bloomberg School of Public Health

body.text: Facilitator Preparation

items.text: Review all pre-recorded videos and supplementary materials.
Ensure you have the facilitator slides.
Familiarize yourself with key concepts and discussion prompts.
Ensure technical setup for video playback and audience participation.

body.text: Small Group Discussion Tips

Many student participants may not turn their cameras on in the zoom discussions, and we do not suggest that you request they be turned on. We recommend starting with a welcome and using names when inviting responses to help create a more personal and engaging atmosphere. Admit that you may pronounce names incorrectly, and that you would like to be corrected if an error is made. Inform them that you will start by asking everyone to respond to the first question and state the question again (to give some additional time for the first responder to have a response).

Begin with the provided starter question and go around the room, inviting each person to share. Feel free to gently prompt quiet participants or encourage responses in the chat if needed. Alternate the discussion questions between having everyone respond and having volunteers respond. Do not be afraid of silence... this is when thinking is happening. After a period, state the question again, thank the audience for the time of reflection, and invite responses either voluntarily or in the chat.
LEARNING SESSION GUIDES
Introduction to Public Health
Ideal Course for Introduction to Public Health: Risk & Protective Factors

This lesson is designed for an introductory course that provides a broad overview of the field of public health, with a focus on promoting health and preventing disease within communities. Students would explore core public health concepts, including epidemiology, health policy, environmental health, social and behavioral sciences, and global health. Emphasis would be placed on health equity, Indigenous health perspectives, and the unique health challenges and strengths of Native communities.

This lecture fits into the introduction to public health course described above as it expands the discussion about risk and protective factors to include those related to injury prevention. This session also has a specific focus on environmental health risk factors in injury prevention.
Lesson Outline: Introduction to Public Health: Risk & Protective Factors

items.text: Use the “Introduction to Public Health: Risk & Protective Factors”Facilitator Slides for the Welcome & Introduction (5 min)
Introduce yourself, how the lesson fits into the course, and review the learning objectives.

children.text: The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference: After this introduction lecture to injury prevention, you will be able to:

items.text: Define injury
Describe how injury compares with other leading causes of death
Describe injury epidemiology for both intentional and unintentional injuries.

body.text: b. Conduct the introduction activity: Zoom Poll OR Waterfall Multiple Choice
The activity is outlined in the slides, but here is the activity description.

THIS NEEDS TO BE SET UP AS A ZOOM POLL. As the details for setting up a zoom poll (or alternative remote meeting software) may change with time and updates, this will require some preparation ahead of the lecture. Set up the poll using this multiple-choice question (also on the slide).

Which of the following is an example of an environmental risk factor that can increase the likelihood of unintentional injury? A) Adequate street lighting in residential areas B) Presence of broken or uneven sidewalks C) Use of safety gates at the top of stairs D) Installation of smoke alarms in all bedrooms

Give a few moments for everyone in the class to respond to the poll and encourage guessing (if the answer is not known) as the responses will be anonymous. Close the poll and present the results. Provide the correct answer of “B.” Comment on the type of responses given and that everyone should be more familiar with the phrase “environmental risk factor” after the lesson.

An alternative is to invite the students to select an answer and put it in the chat. Use the waterfall approach and have them write their choice but press enter at the same time when you say “go.” After the waterfall, provide the correct answer of “B.” Comment on the type of responses given and that everyone should be more familiar with the phrase “environmental risk factor” after the lesson.

Possible extension activity: If there is good engagement, an extension of the activity would be for the students to enter in their responses to the verbal question “What is a term to describe the other selections, A, C, and D?” Desired response: Protective Factor.

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.
Play the animation. Link to animation is also in the Facilitator Slides: https://wisqars.cdc.gov/animated-leading-causes/

After the animation is done, comment on the chat entries or invite comments again either verbally or in the chat. Encourage responses to when they saw a sharp rise in unintentional injuries and brainstorm what the causes may be. Emphasize there are no “wrong” answers, we are just brainstorming.
Share the information that the rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic.

Transition to the next lecture by indicating one of the ways to mitigate unintentional injuries (the highest cause of death in the US for those 1-44) is to understand and address risk and protective factors.
4. Introduce and play the Recorded Lecture: Risk & Protective Factors (20 min)
The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:

children.text: After this lecture, you will be able to:

items.text: Describe risk factors associated with falls and fires/burns.
Describe protective factors associated with falls and fires/burns.

body.text: 5. Small Group Discussion (20 min)

The discussion questions arealsoin the facilitator slides. If there are breakout rooms, post the discussion questions in the chat prior to joining the breakout sessions [This allows students to see and reference the questions in their breakout rooms.]

items.text: What are some risk factors you see in your community for fires and burns? Do you see any protective factors?
What protective factors do you see in your community for falls? Do you see any risk factors?

Supplementary questions not posted on slide but could be used to facilitate more discussion if needed. o What caught your attention today in the lectures? Why do you think this made an impression on you? o Do you have any suggestions for your community based on the information presented today?

body.text: 6. Wrap-Up Using the Facilitator Slides (5 min)

If there is time, open floor for final questions and reflections. If there were separate breakout sessions, invite a student from each group to summarize some of the discussion points. The Facilitator Slides assist in providing some additional resources and contact details if any student would like to have a follow-up discussion.
Planning, Implementing, and Evaluating Health Promotion Programs
Ideal Course for Planning, Implementing, and Evaluating Health Promotion Programs: Safety in Seconds

A course ideal for this lesson would be a course that provides students with the knowledge and skills to design, carry out, and evaluate community-based health promotion programs. Emphasizing Indigenous worldviews and cultural relevance, students would learn how to work collaboratively with tribal communities to identify health priorities, develop strategies, and measure outcomes.
This lesson plan builds on such a Planning, Implementation and Evaluation course by discussing a specific evaluation of an injury prevention program called Safety in Seconds.
Lesson Outline: Planning, Implementing, and Evaluating Health Promotion Programs: Safety in Seconds

items.text: Use the “Planning, Implementing, and Evaluating Health Promotion Programs: Safety in Seconds”Facilitator Slides for the Welcome & Introduction (5 min)
Introduce yourself, how the lesson fits into the course, and review the learning objectives.

children.text: The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference: After this introduction lecture to injury prevention, you will be able to:

items.text: Define injury
Describe how injury compares with other leading causes of death
Describe injury epidemiology for both intentional and unintentional injuries.

body.text: b. Conduct the introduction activity: Silent Brainstorm
The activity is outlined in the slides, but here is the activity description.
Show the following question on the facilitator slide and read it aloud.
“Do you think using technology might be an effective tool to address a public health problem? Do you know of any examples?”

State you are giving them 45 seconds (allow 30-60 seconds) of silent reflection before you invite them to respond in the chat. This allows introverted thinkers time to gather their thoughts and levels the playing field. After the appropriate time, invite responses and respond appropriately. You may perhaps state that this might be an easier question to answer after the day’s lesson.

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention to when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.
Play the animation. Link to animation is also in the Facilitator Slides: https://wisqars.cdc.gov/animated-leading-causes/

After the animation is done, comment on the chat entries or invite comments again either verbally or in the chat. Encourage responses to when they saw a sharp rise in unintentional injuries and brainstorm what the causes may be. Emphasize there are no “wrong” answers, we are just brainstorming. Share the information that the rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic. Transition to the next recorded lecture slides by introducing the Part II objective slides and stating thatthere are several methods that have been developed and tested to help reduce the numbers of unintentional injuries.
4. Introduce and play the Recorded Lecture: Safety in Seconds (20 min)
The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:

children.text: After this lecture, you will be able to:

items.text: Be able to describe an educational intervention effort using technology called Safety in Seconds.
Identify the theoretical underpinnings of the technology-based intervention.
Be able to describe the efficacy of using an app-based intervention program.

body.text: 5. Small Group Discussion (20 min)

The discussion questions are also in the facilitator slides. If there are breakout rooms, post the discussion questions in the chat prior to joining the breakout sessions [This allows students to see and reference the questions in their breakout rooms.]

items.text: How does the Safety in Seconds lecture relate to what you are learning in class?
What types of tailored messages do you think would be effective in your community to increase car seat usage?
What ideas do you have for creating a technological intervention to address an injury issue in your community?

body.text: Principles of Health Education
Ideal Course for Principles of Health Education: Home Injury Prevention & CHASE

This lesson plan would fit a foundational course that explores the core principles of health education, including learning theories, health behavior models, and effective communication strategies. Students would learn to design and deliver culturally appropriate health education interventions using both modern and traditional methods, emphasizing respect for tribal values, language, and storytelling in health messaging and education.

This lecture builds on discussions in a Health Education course by focusing on an intervention developed using the Health Belief Model. The intervention includes educational materials for child injury prevention in the home setting.
Lesson Outline: Principles of Health Education: Home Injury Prevention & CHASE

items.text: Use the “Principles of Health Education: Home Injury Prevention”Facilitator Slides for the Welcome & Introduction (5 min)
Introduce yourself, how the lesson fits into the course, and review the learning objectives.

body.text: The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:
b. Conduct the introduction activity: Zoom Chat Storm
The activity is outlined in the slides, but here is the activity description.

Ask everyone to respond immediately in the chat to the prompt “What are the public health issues that you have discussed so far in your studies?” This works because it is rapid and shows the diversity of experiences all at once.
Comment on the variety of responses, or that hopefully the variety of responses will be increased through the introduction of Injury Prevention and Home Injury Prevention issues in today’s lesson.

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention to when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.
Play the animation. Link to animation is also in the Facilitator Slides: https://wisqars.cdc.gov/animated-leading-causes/

After the animation is done, comment on the chat entries or invite comments again either verbally or in the chat. Encourage responses to when they saw a sharp rise in unintentional injuries and brainstorm what the causes may be. Emphasize there are no “wrong” answers, we are just brainstorming.

Transition to playing the next recorded lecture by sharing thatthe rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic, but many unintentional injuries, particularly in the elderly, occur in the home.
4. Introduce and play the Recorded Lecture: Home Injury Prevention & CHASE(20 min)
The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:

children.text: After this lecture, you will be able to:

body.text: 5. Small Group Discussion (20 min)

The discussion questions are also in the facilitator slides. If there are breakout rooms, post the discussion questions in the chat prior to joining the breakout sessions [This allows students to see and reference the questions in their breakout rooms.]

rows: • What connections did you see inthis lecture to your current coursework?
• If you were to design healtheducation materials, whichmodality or method of instruction would you use?
• From your perspective, whatstructural barriers do you think maybe driving home injury risks?

body.text: American Indian Maternal, Infant, Child, and Sexual Health
Ideal Course for American Indian Maternal, Infant, Child, and Sexual Health: Reducing Disparities in Risks for Sleep-Related Infant Deaths

This lesson plan would fit in a course that explores key public health issues affecting American Indian maternal and child health, with a focus on culturally grounded approaches to care, community wellness, and health equity. Ideally, students will be examining historical and contemporary factors influencing health outcomes, including social determinants of health, federal and tribal health policies, and traditional practices prepare students to support maternal and child health initiatives in American Indian communities.
This lesson builds from what would be covered in a Maternal and Child Health course by including injury broadly in the discussion of maternal and child health and doing a deep dive into Safe Sleep.
Lesson Outline: American Indian Maternal, Infant, Child, and Sexual Health: Reducing Disparities in Risks for Sleep-Related Infant Deaths

items.text: Use the “American Indian Maternal, Infant, Child, and Sexual Health: Reducing Disparities in Risks for Sleep-Related Infant Deaths”Facilitator Slides for the Welcome & Introduction (5 min)
Introduce yourself, how the lesson fits into the course, and review the learning objectives.

children.text: The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference: After this introduction lecture to injury prevention, you will be able to:

items.text: Define injury
Describe how injury compares with other leading causes of death
Describe injury epidemiology for both intentional and unintentional injuries.

body.text: b. Conduct the introduction activity: Zoom Shout Out
The activity is outlined in the slides, but here is the activity description.
The slide has the following question written out: “Who taught you to care for children?” Ask them to type the names in the chat. This provides a burst of inspiration and activity.

Transition to playing the recorded lecture using a phrase similar to, “Grateful for the people in our lives who have taught us important skills, we are now going to learn more about keeping people safe and free from life altering injury.”

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.
Play the animation. Link to animation is also in the Facilitator Slides: https://wisqars.cdc.gov/animated-leading-causes/

After the animation is done, comment on the chat entries or invite comments again either verbally or in the chat. Encourage responses to when they saw a sharp rise in unintentional injuries and brainstorm what the causes may be. Emphasize there are no “wrong” answers, we are just brainstorming.

Transition to the next lecture by sharing the information that the rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic. However, in children younger than one, a large number of unintentional injuries are the sleeprelated infant deaths thatwill be addressed in the next recorded lecture.
4. Introduce and play the Recorded Lecture: Reducing Disparities in Risks for Sleep- Related Infant Deaths (20 min)
The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:
5. Small Group Discussion (20 min)

The discussion questions are also in the facilitator slides. If there are breakout rooms, post the discussion questions in the chat prior to joining the breakout sessions [This allows students to see and reference the questions in their breakout rooms.]
Health Services & Policy
Ideal Course for Health Services & Policy: Seat Belt Policy

Ideal for this lesson plan would be an introductory course on health policy development and analysis, exploring how laws and policies impact the health of Indigenous communities. Course topics could include sovereignty, the Indian Health Care Improvement Act, Medicaid, and advocacy for tribal health priorities.
This lecture builds on what would be discussed in a Health Services & Policy Course by focusing on seat belt policy as an evidenced-based intervention to reduce motor vehicle injuries & deaths.
Lesson Outline: Health Services & Policy: Seat Belt Policy

items.text: Use the “Health Services & Policy: Seat Belt PolicyStory”Facilitator Slides for the Welcome & Introduction (5 min)
Introduce yourself, how the lesson fits into the course, and review the learning objectives.

body.text: The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:
b. Conduct the introduction activity: Zoom Waterfall
The activity is outlined in the slides, but here is the activity description.

The "waterfall" activity on Zoom is a simple, engaging way to gather input from all participants at once. You, the facilitator, will pose a question and ask everyone to type their response into the chat box—but not to press "Enter" yet. After giving everyone a moment to type, the facilitator prompts participants to hit "Enter" at the same time, creating a "waterfall" effect of responses flowing into the chat all at once. This technique encourages participation, reduces response bias, and allows quieter voices to be heard equally.
An example script to use for the activity:
"Let’s do a quick waterfall activityto hear from everyone at once. Please take a moment to type your answer to this question in the chat—but don’t press Enter yet:
‘What are some of the policy issues you have discussed so far in your course?’
Once you’ve typed your response, just wait. I’ll count down and then we’ll all hit Enter together to ‘release the waterfall’ of responses.
Ready? 3… 2… 1 … Go!"
Comment if any injury prevention topics show up in the chat. Transition to the lecture by suggesting that the next time you ask this question, perhaps there will be more injury related responses.

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.
Play the animation. Link to animation is also in the Facilitator Slides: https://wisqars.cdc.gov/animated-leading-causes/

After the animation is done, comment on the chat entries or invite comments again either verbally or in the chat. Encourage responses to when they saw a sharp rise in unintentional injuries and brainstorm what the causes may be. Emphasize there are no “wrong” answers, we are just brainstorming. Share the information that the rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic. Transition to the next lecture by indicating that without the Seat Belt Policy Story, unintentional injury deaths would most likely be even higher.
4. Introduce and play the Recorded Lecture: Seat Belt Policy Story (20 min)
The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:
5. Small Group Discussion (20 min)

The discussion questions are also in the facilitator slides. If there are breakout rooms, post the discussion questions in the chat prior to joining the breakout sessions [This allows students to see and reference the questions in their breakout rooms.]
Introduction to Wellness
Ideal Course for Introduction to Wellness: Healthy Relationships

A course suited for this lesson plan would introduce students to the concept of holistic wellness, exploring the physical, mental, and emotional dimensions of health. Drawing from both Indigenous teachings and contemporary health sciences, students would examine how cultural identity, traditional practices, and community strengths contribute to wellness. Topics include nutrition, physical activity, stress management, and the role of ceremony, relationships, and land in health.

This lecture builds on discussions in an Introduction to Wellness course by providing a basic introduction to injury prevention and giving specific examples of two programs (Azhe’é Bidziil (Strong Fathers) and Hastiin Bidziil (Strong Man) that foster healthy relationships and prevent violence.
Lesson Outline: Introduction to Wellness: Healthy Relationships

items.text: Use the “Introduction to Wellness: Healthy Relationships”Facilitator Slides for the Welcome & Introduction (5 min)
Introduce yourself, how the lesson fits into the course, and review the learning objectives.

body.text: b. Conduct the introduction activity: Zoom Emoji Response Activity
The activity is outlined in the slides, but here is the activity description.

Understanding that relationships and violence can be difficult subjects to talk about based on personal experiences, this activity allows these feelings to be shared, in a fun way. The question “How are you feeling about today’s topic?” and the direction “Put an emoji in the chat.” are on the slide. Invite participation, allow for multiple emoji’s to be shared, and share your own emoji representing your feelings about today’s topic.

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.
Play the animation. Link to animation is also in the Facilitator Slides: https://wisqars.cdc.gov/animated-leading-causes/

After the animation is done, comment on the chat entries or invite comments again either verbally or in the chat. Encourage responses to when they saw a sharp rise in unintentional injuries and brainstorm what the causes may be. Emphasize there are no “wrong” answers, we are just brainstorming.

Share the information that the rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic. Transition to today’s lecture as it will address intentional injuries caused by violence and how to prevent this violence through healthy relationships.
4. Introduce and play the Recorded Lecture: Healthy Relationships (20 min)
The learning objectives are in the slides, but here they are for your reference:
5. Small Group Discussion (20 min)

The discussion questions are also in the facilitator slides. If there are breakout rooms, post the discussion questions in the chat prior to joining the breakout sessions [This allows students to see and reference the questions in their breakout rooms.]
Environmental Health
Ideal Course for Environmental Health: Safe Systems

A course focusing on the impact of the environment on human health would be ideal for this lesson plan. The course would explore environmental hazards, pollution, climate change, and resource management on tribal lands. Students would learn about environmental justice, tribal environmental programs, and culturally appropriate strategies for addressing environmental health issues.

This lecture builds on content in an Environmental Health course by discussing the environmental and structural approach to road safety in the Safe System Approach that aims to prevent motor vehicle and pedestrian fatalities.
Lesson Outline: Environmental Health: Safe Systems

items.text: Use the “Environmental Health: Safe Systems” Facilitator Slides for the Welcome & Introduction (5 min)
Introduce yourself, how the lesson fits into the course, and review the learning objectives.

body.text: b. Conduct the introduction activity: Zoom One Word Popcorn
The activity is outlined in the slides, but here is the activity description.

Ask the question that is on the slide, “What is one word that describes roads on the Navajo Nation?” Invite participants take turns unmuting and quickly saying their word. You, as the facilitator, could call on names, or you can have the current speaker call on the next person. This way, everyone participates briefly, and it sets a quick rhythm.

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.
Play the animation.
Link to animation is also in the Facilitator Slides: https://wisqars.cdc.gov/animated-leading-causes/

After the animation is done, comment on the chat entries or invite comments again either verbally or in the chat. Encourage responses to when they saw a sharp rise in unintentional injuries and brainstorm what the causes may be. Emphasize there are no “wrong” answers, we are just brainstorming. When appropriate, provide the answer that the rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic.

Transition to the next recorded lecture by mentioning about 40,000 unintentional deaths each year are because of motor vehicle crashes. The next lecture will address what environmental efforts could mitigate the danger of being on the roads.
4. Introduce and play the Recorded Lecture: “Safe Systems”(20 min)

5. Small Group Discussion (20 min)

Public Health Research Methods
Ideal Course for Public Health Research Methods: Research Study Designs in Injury & Violence Prevention

This lesson plan would fit a course that introduces students to key concepts and methods used in public health research, including study design, data collection, and basic data analysis. Emphasis would be placed on ethical research practices and Indigenous research methodologies, such as community-based participatory research (CBPR). Students would learn how to critically review research studies and design small-scale research projects that are respectful and responsive to tribal communities.

This lecture builds on a Research Methods course by introducing Injury Prevention as a Public Health discipline and giving specific examples of research study designs used in injury and violence prevention studies.
Lesson Outline: Public Health Research Methods: Research Study Design in Injury & Violence Prevention

body.text: b. Conduct the introduction activity: Zoom Show and Tell

The facilitator slide has the following question written out: “What do you see in your surroundings right now that represents your interest in public health?” Ask the students to either post a picture or write out a description in the chat. They are invited to describe why, if they choose, but they can leave the reason up to our imagination. This activity will provide you with connection and personal, visual elements and interactions with the students. We encourage you to post a picture.

items.text: Play the Recorded Lecture: Injury 101 (20 min)
Conduct the CDC Animated Leading Causes of Death activity. (5 min)

body.text: Explain to the students that you will play for them an animated chart showing the leading causes of death for ages 1-44 years in the U.S. This is to help them understand how unintentional injury has evolved over time as a major public health issue, and that you want them to type in their reactions to the viewing in the chat.
Askthem to pay particular attention when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.

Share the information that the rise of unintentional injuries in 2010 was related to the opioid epidemic. Transition to the recorded lecture by stating researchers in Injury Prevention design studies to reduce the unintentional and intentional injuries.

Community Health Assessment and Planning
Ideal Course for Community Health Assessment and Planning: Addressing the Opioid Epidemic

An ideal course for this lesson plan would be a course where students learn how to conduct community health assessments using both qualitative and quantitative methods, including surveys, interviews, and focus groups. The course would emphasize asset-based and participatory approaches that engage tribal leaders, health workers, and community members.

This lecture fits into a community health assessment and planning course as it introduces an injury prevention community–based project. The project involved identifying opioid related deaths as a public health problem, risk factors associated with opioid deaths, and the lecture describes the implementation of an intervention.
Lesson Outline: Community Health Assessment and Planning: Addressing the Opioid Epidemic

Askthem to pay particular attention to when a sharp rise in unintentional injuries occurs and to think of possible reasons forthis increase.

Mental Health Issues in Public Health
Background Material for Alcohol & Substance Use

A course that explores mental and behavioral health within a public health framework, focusing on Native communities, would be a good fit for this lesson plan. Topics could include historical and intergenerational trauma, substance use, suicide prevention, and resilience. Students would examine culturally responsive prevention and intervention strategies, as well as community-based programs that integrate traditional healing and mental wellness practices.

This lecture adds to a Mental Health course discussion by looking at alcohol and drug use from an injury prevention perspective after an introduction to injury prevention as a public health discipline.
Lesson Outline: Mental Health Issues in Public Health: Alcohol & Substance Use
